---
title: Tutorial template
description: A learning-oriented layout for narrative, hands-on docs that give readers an immediate win.
---

Use this layout when you want to guide someone through a first success and help them start building a mental model of your product. The reader should finish with a concrete win — not a menu of options or a deep dive into architecture.

<Info>
  The examples on this page and the other **Templates** pages use a fictional **Coffee Shop Ordering API**. The same product scenario appears in every template so you can compare how structure, tone, and code change by documentation type.
</Info>

## When to use it

Pick the tutorial layout for onboarding: a new user, a learning path, or any page where one small outcome matters more than exhaustive coverage.

| Use a tutorial when… | Use something else when… |
| --- | --- |
| The reader has never done this before | They need one specific task → [How-to](/templates/how-to) |
| You want one guaranteed success path | They need field-by-field lookup → [Reference](/templates/reference) |
| Choices and tradeoffs would slow them down | They need background and *why* → [Explanation](/templates/explanation) |

## The goal

Provide a narrative, hands-on experience that gives the reader an immediate win and helps them begin building a mental model of the system.

## The tone

Encouraging, authoritative, and instructional. Tell them what to do next. Do not offer alternatives, deep explanations, or production hardening on this page.

## Recommended layout

Structure every tutorial with these sections:

1. **Introduction** — State exactly what the reader will accomplish by the end.
2. **Prerequisites** — List tools, keys, or setup they need before starting.
3. **Step-by-step instructions** — Chronological steps that lead to a successful result.
4. **Next steps** — Point to a how-to, reference, or explanation once the win is achieved.

Use `<Steps>` for long procedures. Keep code blocks copy-paste ready.

## Copy this template

Copy the skeleton below into a new `.mdx` file and replace placeholder paths and content with your own.

````mdx title="docs/create-your-first-coffee-order.mdx"
---
title: Create your first coffee order
description: Place your first espresso order using the Coffee Shop Ordering API.
---

This page guides you through setting up your environment and placing your very first espresso order.

## Prerequisites

- A Barista API key
- `curl` or any HTTP client

## Steps

<Steps>
  <Step title="Get your Barista API key">
    Copy your sandbox key from your project's developer console.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Construct the payload">
    Create a JSON object for a simple hot espresso:

    ```json
    {
      "item": "espresso",
      "size": "single"
    }
    ```
  </Step>
  <Step title="Send the request">
    Run this command in your terminal:

    ```bash
    curl -X POST https://api.coffeeshop.dev/v1/orders \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" \
      -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
      -d '{"item": "espresso", "size": "single"}'
    ```

    You should receive a `201 Created` response with an `order_id`.
  </Step>
</Steps>

## Next steps

- [Customize drinks with milk alternatives](/path/to/how-to)
- [Orders API reference](/path/to/reference)
````

## Example: Create your first coffee order

Below is how a tutorial page might read for our fictional coffee API. Notice the narrow path, imperative steps, and a single celebratory outcome.

### Introduction

This page guides you through setting up your environment and placing your very first espresso order using the Coffee Shop Ordering API.

### Prerequisites

- A Barista API key (use `test_key_demo` for sandbox requests)
- `curl` or any HTTP client

### Step-by-step instructions

<Steps>
  <Step title="Get your Barista API key">
    Copy your sandbox key from your project's developer console. For this walkthrough, you can use the mock key `test_key_demo`.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Construct the payload">
    Create a JSON object for a simple hot espresso:

    ```json
    {
      "item": "espresso",
      "size": "single"
    }
    ```
  </Step>
  <Step title="Send the request">
    Run this command in your terminal:

    ```bash
    curl -X POST https://api.coffeeshop.dev/v1/orders \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer test_key_demo" \
      -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
      -d '{"item": "espresso", "size": "single"}'
    ```

    You should receive a `201 Created` response with an `order_id`. Your virtual espresso is brewing.
  </Step>
</Steps>

### Next steps

- Customize an order with milk alternatives → see the [How-to template](/templates/how-to)
- Look up every request field → see the [Reference template](/templates/reference)

## How tutorials treat code

In a tutorial, code blocks are **complete, copy-paste solutions**. The reader should not edit placeholders mid-flow or compare variants. Save surgical edits for how-to guides, field tables for reference, and architecture diagrams for explanations.

## Next steps

- [How-to template](/templates/how-to) — Task-oriented pages for readers who know the basics
- [Reference template](/templates/reference) — Lookup pages for APIs, config, and commands
- [Explanation template](/templates/explanation) — Background on architecture and design decisions
